title: Chameleon Colors

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-02-brain-teasers.md

setup.narrative: A remote island has chameleons of three colors: 13 red, 15 green, 17 blue. When two chameleons of DIFFERENT colors meet, both change to the THIRD color.
rules: When a red and a green meet: both become blue (R - 1, G - 1, B + 2).
When a red and a blue meet: both become green (R - 1, G + 2, B - 1).
When a green and a blue meet: both become red (R + 2, G - 1, B - 1).
setup.question: Can the chameleons ever ALL become the same color?

learningObjectives: Compute residues mod 3 of the three color counts and show they're permanently distinct.
Recognize that 'all same color' requires two of the residues to coincide -- which is impossible.
Generalize the principle: invariants under multiple move types often live in mod-3 (or higher) classes.

stages.label: Why this is a state-machine problem

options.text: Find an invariant under all three meeting types -- if 'all same color' violates the invariant, it's unreachable.
Probability: with random meetings, eventually all-same occurs.

stages.explanation: We need to either construct a sequence of meetings that produces all-same, or prove no such sequence exists. The right tool for impossibility proofs in state machines is finding an invariant.

stages.label: Compute initial residues
stages.explanation: 13 = 4*3 + 1, so 13 mod 3 = 1. Similarly 15 mod 3 = 0 and 17 mod 3 = 2. Initial residue triple = (1, 0, 2).

stages.label: How meetings affect residues
stages.prompt: Each meeting changes two counts by -1 and one count by +2. What does this do to residues mod 3?

options.text: Two residues each decrease by 1 (mod 3), and one residue increases by 2 (mod 3) -- but +2 = -1 mod 3, so ALL THREE residues decrease by 1 mod 3.
The MULTISET of residues changes unpredictably.

stages.explanation: Key insight: -1 and +2 are the SAME mod 3. So every meeting subtracts 1 from each of the three residues simultaneously. The MULTISET of residues is preserved (just cyclically shifted).

stages.label: Why all-same is impossible
stages.prompt: If the three counts ever became (N, 0, 0) -- all same color -- what would the multiset of residues mod 3 be?

options.text: {N mod 3, 0, 0} -- two zeros. But the initial multiset is {0, 1, 2} with three DISTINCT residues. So unreachable.

stages.explanation: (N, 0, 0) has multiset {N mod 3, 0, 0} -- contains TWO zeros. The initial multiset is {0, 1, 2} -- all DISTINCT. Since meetings preserve the multiset (modulo cyclic shift, all three residues remain distinct), (N, 0, 0) is unreachable forever.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Can the chameleons ever become all the same color?

options.text: No -- the three residues mod 3 of the color counts remain permanently distinct.
Yes -- given enough time it'll happen.

stages.explanation: Impossible. The mod-3 invariant rules out the target state forever. (This is a classic application of modular arithmetic to a state-machine problem.)

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: What initial counts WOULD allow reaching all-same color?
What if the rule were 'two same-color chameleons turn into one of each remaining color'? Would all-same be reachable?
Why is mod 3 the right modulus for this problem -- not mod 2 or mod 4?
